summary:
Rory is a 26-29 year old medical student who has engineered a highly systematised approach to food that prioritises efficiency over engagement. Having returned to university after working as a dentist for four years, he operates with the pragmatism of someone who views food primarily as fuel for a demanding schedule. His weekday eating is almost entirely automated through a meal prep service he's used for three years, combined with repetitive lunch preparation that he describes as making 'the same thing for months at a time until I get sick of it.' This systematic approach extends to his delivery habits, where he's deeply embedded in the Uber ecosystem not just for food, but for transport, travel booking, and other services, creating a comprehensive platform lock-in that would be difficult for competitors to break.

What's particularly striking about Rory is the contrast between his articulate understanding of cooking ('you're chopping the vegetables and you're cooking the meat and you're putting all together, you're making the sauce') and his complete avoidance of it in practice ('almost never'). He only cooks when entertaining others, perhaps 'once every two, three months.' His food choices are driven by convenience and habit rather than pleasure or exploration, though he becomes more social and indulgent on weekends. Despite his systematic approach, he maintains a relaxed attitude toward spending and doesn't track his food budget closely, suggesting someone who has optimised for time and mental energy rather than cost.
personality_notes: Articulate, systematic, and pragmatic. Speaks in a measured, analytical way with clear explanations. Shows the efficiency-focused mindset of someone balancing demanding medical studies with part-time dental work. Not particularly expressive about food or emotional topics.
food_relationship: Food is primarily functional fuel managed through systematic meal prep services and repetitive habits, with minimal cooking or culinary engagement except for occasional social occasions.

key_moments.description: Detailed explanation of his 3-year relationship with Prep Kitchen meal service
key_moments.significance: Reveals the depth of his systematised approach to food - this isn't a recent convenience but a long-term lifestyle choice that defines his entire weekday eating pattern

key_moments.description: Defines cooking very specifically but admits to 'almost never' doing it
key_moments.significance: Shows the gap between understanding and practice - he knows what cooking is but has completely opted out of it in his daily life

key_moments.description: Explains comprehensive Uber ecosystem integration across transport, travel, and food
key_moments.significance: Demonstrates how platform lock-in works in practice - switching would require replacing multiple integrated services, not just food delivery

what_not_said: Any emotional connection to food or cooking
Concerns about nutrition or health despite being a medical student
Interest in food culture or trying new cuisines
Any mention of food bringing joy or pleasure beyond weekend social occasions
initial_hypotheses: Represents the 'optimised efficiency' segment - users who have systematically removed food decision-making from their daily routine
Platform ecosystem effects are stronger than individual app features for retention
Medical/healthcare students may be particularly prone to functional food relationships due to time pressures
Meal prep services may be competing more directly with delivery than traditional cooking
questions_for_coding: How do systematic meal prep users interact with delivery services differently?
What role does delivery play when primary food systems (meal prep) break down?
How does professional/academic identity influence food relationship?
What would it take to break strong platform ecosystem lock-in?
diary_summary: Rory's 7-day diary perfectly aligns with his interview self-description, showing a highly consistent pattern of skipping breakfast daily, relying heavily on his meal prep service for dinners, and having minimal variety in his eating routine.
say_do_gaps: No significant gaps found - his diary behaviour closely matches his interview descriptions, particularly around never eating breakfast and relying on meal prep services
His claim of ordering delivery 'about once a week' wasn't reflected in the diary week (0 delivery orders), but this could be normal weekly variation
